expanded_description: Master the proportionality theorems that reveal hidden relationships within triangles: how parallel lines split sides proportionally and how angle bisectors create precise segment ratios.

core_concept.explanation: When a line parallel to one side of a triangle intersects the other two sides, it divides those sides proportionally. Similarly, an angle bisector divides the opposite side in the ratio of the adjacent sides. These theorems turn geometric configurations into solvable proportional equations.

image.alt: Two triangles: one with a parallel line splitting sides, one with an angle bisector splitting the opposite side
image.caption: Two distinct mechanisms that generate proportional segments inside triangles

sections.introduction: The Triangle Proportionality Theorem is one of the most frequently used tools in similarity. It states that a line parallel to one side of a triangle creates proportional segments on the other two sides.

key_concept.definition: If a line parallel to one side of a triangle intersects the other two sides, then it divides those sides proportionally: $\overline{DE} \parallel \overline{BC} \Rightarrow \dfrac{AD}{DB} = \dfrac{AE}{EC}$.
key_concept.context: The parallel line creates a smaller similar triangle ($\triangle ADE \sim \triangle ABC$), which forces the side segments to maintain the same ratio.

image.alt: Triangle ABC with line DE parallel to BC, segments labeled AD, DB, AE, EC
image.caption: The parallel segment splits the two sides into matching ratios

principles.statement: The theorem uses the split segments ($\frac{AD}{DB}$), not the whole sides. However, $\frac{AD}{AB} = \frac{AE}{AC}$ is also true due to similarity.
principles.application: Always match the ratio structure: segment-to-segment OR part-to-whole. Mixing them causes errors.
principles.analogy: Like cutting two identical ropes at the same fractional point: the remaining pieces maintain the original length ratio.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify corresponding split segments from the parallel line configuration
Substitute known values into the proportion

worked_examples.title: Algebraic Application
worked_examples.concept_applied: Triangle Proportionality Theorem with algebraic expressions

sections.introduction: Proportionality works both ways: if sides are split proportionally, the cutting line must be parallel. This extends to any set of parallel lines intersecting transversals.

key_concept.definition: Converse: If a line divides two sides of a triangle proportionally ($\frac{AD}{DB} = \frac{AE}{EC}$), then it is parallel to the third side. Three Parallel Lines Theorem: If three or more parallel lines intersect two transversals, they divide the transversals proportionally.
key_concept.context: The converse lets you prove lines are parallel without angle measurements. The three-line theorem applies to trapezoids, ladders, and architectural grids.

image.alt: Triangle showing converse setup and three parallel lines cutting two transversals
image.caption: Proportional splits guarantee parallelism; multiple parallels scale transversals uniformly

principles.application: Use coordinate geometry or given lengths to verify ratios, then conclude parallelism for further proofs or constructions.

principles.statement: If $l_1 \parallel l_2 \parallel l_3$ intersect transversals $t_1$ and $t_2$, then $\frac{\text{segment}_1}{\text{segment}_2} = \frac{\text{segment}_3}{\text{segment}_4}$.
principles.application: Solve for missing segments in ladder problems, fence posts, or city grid layouts where parallel spacing is constant.
principles.example: Parallel streets cut avenues at intervals 2, 4, 6. A crossing street cuts at intervals $x, 8, 12$. Ratio $2:4 = x:8 \Rightarrow x=4$.

categories.description: Match the diagram configuration to the correct proportionality rule.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Single triangle with one parallel cut Ôćĺ Triangle Proportionality. Two triangles sharing a vertex with parallel bases Ôćĺ Similarity ($\frac{\text{part}}{\text{whole}}$). Three+ parallel lines cutting two lines Ôćĺ Three Parallel Lines Theorem.

examples.explanation: Rungs are parallel, side rails are transversals. Use Three Parallel Lines Theorem to find missing rung spacing.

image.alt: Ladder diagram with parallel rungs cutting two side rails
image.caption: Parallel rungs create proportional segments along the rails

sections.introduction: An angle bisector doesn't just split an angleÔÇöit splits the opposite side into segments proportional to the adjacent sides.

key_concept.definition: If a ray bisects an angle of a triangle, it divides the opposite side into segments proportional to the other two sides: $\dfrac{BD}{DC} = \dfrac{AB}{AC}$.
key_concept.context: Unlike the parallel line theorem, this relies on angle equality, not parallelism. The ratio compares the split segments of the opposite side to the full lengths of the adjacent sides.

image.alt: Triangle with angle bisector from vertex A to side BC, splitting it into BD and DC
image.caption: The bisector splits the opposite side in the same ratio as the adjacent sides

principles.statement: Angle bisector ratio: $\frac{\text{segment}_1}{\text{segment}_2} = \frac{\text{adjacent side}_1}{\text{adjacent side}_2}$. This is NOT the same as the parallel line theorem's structure.
principles.application: Identify the bisected angle, locate the opposite side split, and match segments to their adjacent full sides.
principles.analogy: Like balancing a seesaw: the split point on the opposite side shifts closer to the shorter adjacent side to maintain proportional balance.

worked_examples.title: Angle Bisector Segment Calculation
worked_examples.problem: In $\triangle KLM$, $\angle K$ is bisected by $\overline{KN}$, intersecting $\overline{LM}$ at $N$. $KL = 14$, $KM = 21$, and $LM = 20$. Find $LN$ and $NM$.

step_by_step.explanation: Express segments in terms of a variable using the ratio
Use total length to solve for $x$

worked_examples.concept_applied: Angle Bisector Theorem with ratio partitioning

sections.introduction: Proportionality theorems share a common workflow: identify the configuration, set up the correct ratio structure, and solve. Use this systematic approach to avoid mixing theorems.

process_flow.description: Look for parallel line markers ($\parallel$), angle bisector arcs ($\cong$ angle marks), or three+ parallel lines.
process_flow.result: Theorem selection: Parallel Ôćĺ Proportionality; Bisector Ôćĺ Angle Bisector; Multiple parallels Ôćĺ Transversal Theorem

process_flow.description: Label all known lengths. For parallel lines: use split segments or part-to-whole. For bisectors: split segments vs. full adjacent sides.
process_flow.mechanism: Precise correspondence prevents ratio inversion errors

process_flow.description: Set up equation, cross-multiply, isolate variable. For algebraic expressions, verify solutions yield positive lengths.
Check that the solution maintains the ratio, fits within segment totals, and satisfies the original diagram constraints.

classifications.value: Mixing theorem structures or inverting ratios
characteristics: Using whole sides for angle bisector theorem
Applying bisector theorem to parallel line diagrams
classifications.behavior: Always draw arrows from ratio numerator to denominator on the diagram. Verify both sides of the proportion represent the same type of measurement.
classifications.note: Parallel lines = segments on same side compare to each other. Angle bisector = segments on opposite side compare to full adjacent sides.

summary: The Triangle Proportionality Theorem states that a line parallel to one side of a triangle divides the other two sides proportionally ($\frac{AD}{DB} = \frac{AE}{EC}$). Its converse allows proving parallelism from proportional splits. The Three Parallel Lines Theorem extends this to multiple parallels cutting transversals. The Triangle Angle Bisector Theorem states that an angle bisector divides the opposite side proportionally to the adjacent sides ($\frac{BD}{DC} = \frac{AB}{AC}$). Mastering these theorems requires precise identification of the configuration, correct ratio structure setup, and careful algebraic solving. Always verify that solutions yield positive lengths and maintain the original proportional relationships.
